content:
# Mark Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 41r–41v (PDF pages 82–83) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome went out and bought burial spices so they could go and anoint Jesus's body.

² Very early on Sunday morning, just after sunrise, they headed for the tomb.

³ On the way they were asking each other, "Who's going to roll that stone away from the entrance for us?"

⁴ But when they looked up, they saw that the stone — which was massive — had already been rolled aside.

⁵ They went into the tomb and saw a young man sitting on the right side, dressed in a white robe. They were stunned.

⁶ "Don't be alarmed," he said. "You're looking for Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen. He is not here. Look — this is where they put him.

⁷ Now go and tell his disciples — and Peter — that he is going ahead of you to Galilee. You will see him there, just as he told you."

⁸ The women rushed out and ran from the tomb, shaking with shock and terror. They said nothing to anyone — because they were afraid.

---

**[This is where the Gospel of Mark ends in our earliest and best manuscripts — Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus. Verses 9-20 (the "Longer Ending") appear in later manuscripts but are not part of Mark's original text. They were added by later scribes who found Mark's abrupt ending unsatisfying.]**

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The most shocking ending in the Bible:** Mark's Gospel ends mid-sentence — with a conjunction ("for"). The women flee. They tell no one. Fear wins. And the book just... stops. No resurrection appearances. No Great Commission. No ascension. Just an empty tomb, a terrified silence, and a dangling "for." It is the most daring, provocative ending in all of ancient literature.

2. **Why the ending works:** Mark has been training us for this moment. Throughout the Gospel, those commanded to be silent speak (1:45; 7:36), and now those commanded to speak are silent (16:8). The messianic secret has been inverted. The pattern is broken. And the reader is left with the question: Will you do what they couldn't?

3. **"And Peter" (v. 7):** Two of the most gracious words in the New Testament. Peter denied Jesus three times. He wept bitterly. And now the risen Christ sends a specific, personal message: "Tell the disciples — *and Peter*." He is not forgotten. He is not excluded. The denial is not the end of his story.

4. **The young man in white (v. 5):** The last young man we met in Mark's Gospel was running away naked from Jesus's arrest (14:51-52). Now a young man appears clothed in white at the empty tomb. Shame has been replaced by glory. Terror has been replaced by proclamation. Whether Mark intends these as the same person is unknowable, but the literary parallel is unmistakable.

5. **Why verses 9-20 aren't original:** The evidence is overwhelming. (1) Sinaiticus and Vaticanus — our two best manuscripts — both end at 16:8. (2) The vocabulary of 16:9-20 contains numerous words found nowhere else in Mark. (3) The style shifts dramatically. (4) The content summarizes resurrection appearances from Luke, John, and Acts rather than presenting Markan material. (5) Early church fathers (Eusebius, Jerome) noted that the passage was absent from the best copies available to them. These verses are valuable as early Christian tradition, but they are not Mark.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
